metadata.title: Influence: Science and Practice (Cialdini) — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Cialdini shows we judge correct behavior by watching what others do. Social proof, like canned laughter, works even when we know it is fake. Liking helped one salesman become the world's greatest car salesman. We accept a fake doctor's endorsement because the authority cue overrides our knowledge.

artifactLines: Cialdini shows we judge correct behavior by watching what others do.
Social proof, like canned laughter, works even when we know it is fake.
Liking helped one salesman become the world's greatest car salesman.
We accept a fake doctor's endorsement because the authority cue overrides our knowledge.

beats.narration_text: Cialdini's point that we determine correct behavior by watching others, so canned laughter works despite being obviously fake, and that the world's greatest car salesman leaned on liking, makes me want to catch these levers in the wild. Help me: (1) identify which principle each example (canned laughter, the fake-doctor endorsement, the salesman) is exploiting; (2) explain why social proof works even when we know it's manufactured; (3) design one small defense against each of these three principles.
